race.name: Maratona dles Dolomites
race.tagline: Seven Dolomite passes. 9,000 riders chosen by lottery from 31,000 applicants. The most oversubscribed cycling event on Earth.

vitals.location: La Villa / Corvara, Alta Badia, South Tyrol, Italy
vitals.registration: Lottery entry ONLY via maratonadlesdolomites.it. Applications typically open October-December. Results announced January/February. Entry: EUR180. No guaranteed entry — pure luck.
vitals.aid_stations: Feed zones at each pass summit — fully stocked with Italian pastries, fruit, energy food, water, cola. The Dolomite feed zones are legendary for quality.
vitals.cutoff_time: Strict cutoffs at each pass. Riders behind schedule are diverted to shorter routes.
distance_options: Maratona (long): 138 km (86 mi), 4,230 m gain — 7 passes
Medio (medium): 106 km (66 mi), 3,090 m gain — 5 passes
Sellaronda (short): 55 km (34 mi), 1,780 m gain — 4 passes

climate.description: Early July in the Dolomites brings warm valley temperatures (20-28C) and significantly cooler conditions at the 2,200m+ pass summits (8-15C). The Dolomites are notorious for rapid weather changes: clear morning skies can give way to violent afternoon thunderstorms with virtually no warning. The rocky Dolomite towers create their own microclimate.
challenges: Summit temperatures 10-15C cooler than valley floors
Afternoon thunderstorms build rapidly — lightning exposure on exposed passes
Rain turns descents treacherous with 9,000 riders on narrow mountain roads
Early start (6:30 AM) means cold first hour before the sun warms the valleys

terrain.surface: Italian provincial roads — well-maintained tarmac through the Dolomites. All paved. The road surfaces are excellent, resurfaced regularly for the high volume of summer cycling tourism.
features: Passo Campolongo (1,875m) — crossed twice, the opener and a late revisit
Passo Pordoi (2,239m) — the highest pass, long sustained climb with stunning Sella massif views
Passo Sella (2,240m) — short, steep, spectacular. The Sassolungo towers above
Passo Gardena (2,121m) — steep ramps through the heart of Ladinia
Passo Falzarego (2,105m) — exposed climbing beneath the Lagazuoi war tunnels
Passo di Giau (2,236m) — the crux: 9.9 km at 9.3%, pitches of 14%. The hardest climb of the day.
Passo Valparola (2,192m) — final pass before the descent home to Corvara

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: The most oversubscribed cycling event in the world. 31,000+ apply; 9,000 ride. Seven Dolomite passes through UNESCO World Heritage scenery. Lottery-only entry since demand overwhelmed capacity decades ago.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: prestige=5 and cultural_impact=5: this is Italian cycling's crown jewel, the most iconic gran fondo in the world. adventure=5 for UNESCO Dolomite scenery that defies description. logistics=2 reflects the double barrier — lottery entry AND remote Alta Badia location. length=3 at 138 km because the distance is moderate; the climbing makes it hard.

logistics.explanation: Two significant barriers. First: you have to win a lottery. 31,000+ people apply for 9,000 spots. You might apply for years before getting in. Second: Alta Badia is remote. The nearest major airports are Venice (2.5-3 hours), Innsbruck (2 hours), and Munich (4 hours). Corvara is a small village in a dead-end valley with limited accommodation that gets swallowed by 9,000 cyclists and their entourages. You need to plan this trip 6-12 months in advance — if you win the lottery.

length.explanation: 138 km (86 miles) for the full Maratona route is moderate distance. This is not an ultra. The challenge is purely vertical — 4,230m of climbing across 7 passes means you're either going up or going down for the entire ride. Average finishing times are 6-9 hours. The medium (106 km) and short (55 km) options provide accessible alternatives that still deliver Dolomite magic.

technicality.explanation: All paved throughout. Italian mountain road surfaces are excellent — resurfaced regularly for the lucrative cycling tourism industry. The technical demands are limited to descending on narrow roads with 9,000 other riders. The climbs are pure fitness tests. Bring a road bike with compact gearing.

elevation.explanation: 4,230 meters of climbing across 7 named passes. The Passo di Giau alone — 9.9 km at 9.3% with pitches of 14% — would be a defining climb at any event. Then you stack six more passes on top. The Dolomite passes are steep and honest: no false flats, no respite, just 500-800m of climbing per pass, repeated seven times. Your legs never fully recover between climbs.

climate.explanation: Early July in the Dolomites is generally pleasant — warm valleys, cool summits, long daylight hours. But the Dolomites create their own weather. Afternoon thunderstorms can arrive with minimal warning and turn a beautiful day into a survival exercise. The temperature differential between valley (25C) and summit (10C) requires layering strategy. Not the extreme challenge of higher Alpine events, but the storm risk is real.

altitude.explanation: Four passes above 2,200m, with the Pordoi and Sella both reaching 2,240m. This is meaningful altitude — not extreme like the Galibier (2,642m), but sustained time above 2,000m across multiple climbs accumulates an altitude effect. Sea-level riders notice it on the steeper pitches of Giau and Pordoi. The start altitude of 1,568m (Corvara) means you're already elevated before the first climb.

adventure.explanation: The Dolomites are the most visually dramatic cycling landscape on Earth. Not the highest, not the hardest — but the most beautiful. UNESCO World Heritage status exists because these mountains defy description: vertical limestone towers, pink-tinged walls at sunrise, green valleys framed by 3,000m peaks. Each pass reveals a completely different visual world. The Sella massif, the Marmolada glacier, the Cinque Torri — this is cycling through a cathedral of stone.

prestige.explanation: The most oversubscribed cycling event in the world. 31,000+ apply for 9,000 spots. The lottery creates an exclusivity that no other sportive matches — you can't buy your way in, you can't register early, you simply have to be lucky. Founded in 1987 with 166 riders, the Maratona has become the defining Italian gran fondo and arguably the most coveted sportive entry on Earth. Saying 'I rode the Maratona' carries weight in every cycling circle.

race_quality.explanation: Nearly 40 years of organizational refinement from a dedicated local cycling club. Fully closed roads on every pass. Feed zones that serve Italian pastries and espresso alongside gels and bananas. Medical support on every climb. The event logistics for 9,000 riders through narrow mountain villages are handled with Italian precision. The three distance options (55/106/138 km) accommodate different abilities while sharing the same extraordinary roads.

experience.explanation: Cresting each of the seven passes reveals a completely new Dolomite panorama. The Passo di Giau — the crux climb — rewards the suffering with a summit view that has reduced grown adults to tears. The feed zones feel like Italian hospitality stations, not refueling stops. The finish in Corvara, descending from Valparola into the Alta Badia valley with the Dolomite towers glowing in afternoon light, is one of cycling's great moments. The entire day feels like riding through a painting.

community.explanation: The lottery creates a community of the lucky. Riders who have waited years for their number to come up share a particular gratitude and excitement. The event is deeply embedded in Ladin culture — the local minority language of Alta Badia gives the Maratona its distinctive name ('dles Dolomites' is Ladin). The pre-race pasta party, the post-ride celebrations in Corvara's village square, and the Italian cycling culture — espresso before, beer after — create a warm atmosphere.

field_depth.explanation: The lottery entry is random, not merit-based. This means the field is a genuine cross-section: serious racers, weekend warriors, bucket-list tourists, and everything between. The front of the race is fast — Italian club riders and strong amateurs push hard — but the majority of the 9,000-rider field is riding for the experience. Competitive depth is moderate, diluted by the non-selective entry mechanism.

value.explanation: EUR180 for seven Dolomite passes on closed roads with Italian feed zone hospitality is fair. But the hidden cost is the lottery — you pay the entry fee, but you also invest the uncertainty of not knowing if you'll get in. And the trip itself (remote location, peak-season Dolomite accommodation) adds significant expense. The value is in the exclusivity and the experience, not the price-to-distance ratio.

expenses.explanation: Alta Badia in July is peak Dolomite tourism season. Hotels in Corvara and La Villa are limited and pricey — EUR150-300/night for event week. The nearest major airports require 2-4 hour ground transfers. Car rental is almost essential. Tour operator packages run EUR800-2,000+ for 3-5 nights. Budget EUR1,200-2,500 from the US. The Dolomites are not cheap, and the Maratona's demand inflates local pricing further.

biased_opinion.summary: The Maratona dles Dolomites is not just a bike ride. It is a pilgrimage to the most beautiful mountains on Earth, earned through the uncertainty of a lottery and rewarded with seven passes through UNESCO World Heritage scenery that no photograph can capture. Founded in 1987 with 166 riders, it has become the most oversubscribed cycling event in the world — and it deserves every ounce of that demand. The climbs are honest: the Giau will break you, the Pordoi will humble you, and the descent into Corvara will make you forget every moment of suffering.
strengths: UNESCO Dolomite scenery: the most visually spectacular cycling route on Earth
Seven distinct passes, each revealing a completely different mountain panorama
Lottery exclusivity creates genuine prestige — you can't buy your way in
Italian feed zone hospitality: pastries, espresso, and warmth at every summit
weaknesses: Lottery entry: 31,000+ apply for 9,000 spots. You may wait years.
Remote Alta Badia location: 2-4 hours from the nearest major airport
Passo di Giau (9.9 km at 9.3%) is a genuine wall — underprepared riders suffer badly
Afternoon Dolomite thunderstorms can turn the final passes dangerous
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If cycling has a cathedral, it is the Dolomites. And if cycling has a pilgrimage, it is the Maratona. Win the lottery. Train for the Giau. Ride through the most beautiful mountains on the planet. Then understand why 31,000 people apply every year.

final_verdict.one_liner: Seven Dolomite passes. UNESCO World Heritage scenery. 9,000 lottery-selected riders. The most oversubscribed cycling event on Earth.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you can win the lottery and want cycling's most visually spectacular day. Yes if seven Dolomite passes through scenery that defies description is your idea of paradise. Skip if you need guaranteed entry — the lottery is genuine. Skip if remote Italian mountain villages are logistically challenging.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want guaranteed Dolomite access: Sportful Dolomiti Race (500 km away, no lottery). If you want French Alps instead: La Marmotte. If you want Italian hills with guaranteed entry: Nove Colli (Cesenatico, Emilia-Romagna).

logistics.airport: Innsbruck (INN) — 2 hours (closest, limited flights). Venice (VCE) — 2.5-3 hours. Munich (MUC) — 4 hours (best international connections). Verona (VRN) — 3 hours.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Corvara, La Villa, or San Cassiano in Alta Badia — all within the event start/finish area. Hotels are small and limited; book immediately upon winning the lottery. Alternatively, stay in Bressanone or Brunico (30-40 min drive) for more options at lower prices. Many riders use Airbnb/apartment rentals in the valley.
logistics.food: South Tyrolean cuisine: a unique blend of Italian and Austrian. Canederli (bread dumplings), speck, polenta, strudel. Carb loading is effortless. Feed zones are legendary — Italian pastries, fresh fruit, cakes, espresso alongside standard energy food.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event village in La Villa, 1-2 days before. The expo is well-organized and festive. Expect crowds but efficient processing.
logistics.parking: Limited in Corvara/La Villa. Follow event parking instructions carefully. Shuttle services connect parking areas to the start.

history.founder: Societa Ciclistica Alta Badia (on its 10th anniversary)
history.origin_story: Born in 1987 to celebrate the 10th anniversary of the Societa Ciclistica Alta Badia, the first Maratona attracted 166 riders over 7 Dolomite passes. The combination of extraordinary scenery, challenging climbs, and warm Ladin hospitality proved irresistible. Word spread through Italian cycling clubs, then across Europe, then globally. By the 2000s, demand had so far outstripped the 9,000-rider capacity that lottery entry became the only option. Today, 31,000+ apply for the chance to ride. The Maratona has never expanded beyond what the narrow Dolomite roads and small Alta Badia villages can sustainably host.
notable_moments: 1987: Inaugural edition — 166 riders over 7 Dolomite passes
Demand growth forced lottery-only entry — now 31,000+ apply for 9,000 spots
UNESCO World Heritage designation for the Dolomites (2009) added another layer of prestige
Consistently rated the world's most beautiful cycling event by international media
Event name uses Ladin language — the local minority tongue of Alta Badia
history.reputation: The most oversubscribed cycling event on Earth. Not the hardest, not the longest — the most beautiful and the most coveted.

course_description.character: Seven Dolomite passes strung together in a 138 km loop that starts and finishes in Corvara. The route is a continuous series of climbs and descents — there is virtually no flat. Each pass reveals a completely different Dolomite panorama: the Sella massif, the Marmolada glacier, the Cinque Torri, the Tofane. The Passo di Giau is the crux — 9.9 km at 9.3% with pitches of 14% — a genuine wall that comes at km 100 when your legs are already deep in debt.

suffering_zones.label: Opening Passes — Campolongo, Pordoi, Sella
suffering_zones.desc: The first three passes warm you into the Dolomite rhythm. Campolongo (1,875m) is a gentle opener. Pordoi (2,239m) is the first real test — long, sustained, with the Sella massif looming above. Sella (2,240m) is short and steep, with the Sassolungo tower as the backdrop. These three passes consume 60 km and 2,000m of climbing while you still feel fresh.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Three passes, combined 2,000m climbing in 60 km

suffering_zones.label: Gardena + Falzarego
suffering_zones.desc: Passo Gardena (2,121m) is a steep, direct climb through the heart of Ladinia. Falzarego (2,105m) follows, climbing beneath the Lagazuoi — where WWI tunnels are visible in the cliff face above. These middle passes accumulate fatigue without the drama of what's coming.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Two passes, sustained climbing at 7-8% average

suffering_zones.label: Passo di Giau — The Crux
suffering_zones.desc: 9.9 km at 9.3% average with pitches of 14%. This is the hardest climb of the day, arriving at km 100 when your legs are deep in oxygen debt. The gradient is relentless — there is no respite, no false flat, no switchback to catch your breath. The summit view — a 360-degree Dolomite panorama — is the reward. Many riders consider Giau the single hardest climb they've ever done.

suffering_zones.label: Valparola + Home
suffering_zones.desc: The final pass — Valparola (2,192m) — is the last barrier between you and Corvara. After Giau, Valparola feels almost gentle despite its 2,192m summit. The descent into Alta Badia, with the Dolomite towers catching afternoon light, is the emotional climax of the day.

course_description.signature_challenge: The Passo di Giau: 9.9 km at 9.3%, arriving at km 100. The hardest individual climb of the day, on depleted legs, with no place to hide from the gradient. But the real signature is the cumulative effect of seven passes — 4,230m of climbing through the most beautiful mountains in the world.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Dolomite mountain passes — 7 named passes
guide_variables.race_weather: Early July Dolomites — warm valleys (20-28C), cool summits (8-15C), afternoon thunderstorm risk
race_challenges: 4,230m of climbing across 7 named Dolomite passes
Passo di Giau: 9.9 km at 9.3% average — the crux at km 100
Sustained time above 2,000m across multiple passes
Rapid weather changes — afternoon thunderstorms with minimal warning

non_negotiables.requirement: Repeated climbing endurance — multiple 30-45 minute climbs in a single ride
non_negotiables.why: Seven passes means seven distinct climbing efforts with descents between. Train with multiple-climb days: 4-5 significant climbs in a single ride. Your legs need to recover on descents and go again immediately.

The Passo di Giau averages 9.3% with pitches of 14%. Many riders who can climb 6% all day crumble at sustained 9%+. Find the steepest local climb and repeat it. Gearing: bring at minimum a 34x32, ideally 34x34.

non_negotiables.requirement: Full race simulation — 4,000m+ of climbing with nutrition plan
non_negotiables.why: The Maratona's 4,230m of climbing burns 4,000-6,000 calories. Your nutrition strategy needs to sustain 6-9 hours of climbing. Practice eating on 8%+ gradients — it's harder than it sounds when you're gasping for air.
